name: Lit
description: A simple library for building fast, lightweight web components with reactive state and declarative templates

description.value: A simple library for building fast, lightweight web components with reactive state and declarative templates

initial-release.comment: First lit-html prototype released mid-2017, presented at Polymer Summit; LitElement 2.0 released 2019

active-maintenance.comment: Actively maintained; recently joined OpenJS Foundation (October 2025)

reactivity-model.comment: Reactive properties trigger efficient re-renders of only the changed parts of templates

component-model.comment: Components extend LitElement base class; uses decorators for property declarations

rendering-paradigm.comment: Declarative templates using tagged template literals (html``)

state-management-approach.comment: Built-in reactive properties; can integrate with Redux or other state libraries via pwa-helpers

opinionatedness.comment: Minimal and lightweight; focuses on web components standard, flexible integration

backward-compatibility.comment: Strong commitment to backward compatibility; provides upgrade guides between major versions

primary-language.comment: Written in TypeScript (60.5% of codebase); works with JavaScript too

compiles-to.comment: Outputs standard JavaScript; no special compilation required

typescript-support.comment: Written in TypeScript with full decorator support; recommends experimental decorators for now

template-syntax.comment: Uses html`` and css`` tagged template literals

styling-approach.comment: Scoped styles via Shadow DOM; uses css`` tagged template literal

build-required.comment: Can be used without build step via CDN; build recommended for production optimization

computed-values.comment: No built-in computed properties; use getters or reactive controllers

two-way-binding.comment: No built-in two-way binding; uses event-based pattern for form inputs

event-handling.comment: Event binding via @event syntax in templates

conditional-rendering.comment: Ternary operators and conditional templates in lit-html

list-rendering.comment: map() directive and repeat() for efficient list rendering

component-slots.comment: Native <slot> element support via Shadow DOM

teleport-portal.comment: No built-in portal; can use native DOM APIs or third-party solutions

suspense.comment: until() directive for async content loading

error-boundaries.comment: No built-in error boundaries; handle errors in application code

context-provide-inject.comment: @lit-labs/context provides context API for dependency injection

refs-direct-dom-access.comment: @query and @queryAll decorators for direct DOM element access

official-router.comment: @lit-labs/router provides component-oriented routing as reactive controllers

router-name.comment: Official router in Lit Labs; community alternatives include Vaadin Router

nested-routes.comment: Routes controller supports nested route configurations

route-guards.comment: Route enter callbacks for navigation guards

lazy-loading-routes.comment: Dynamic imports supported for lazy route loading

scroll-restoration.comment: Not built into @lit-labs/router; implement manually if needed

built-in-global-state.comment: No official global state solution; integrate with Redux or context

global-state-library.comment: No official library; commonly uses Redux with pwa-helpers or @lit-labs/context

devtools-state-inspection.comment: Lit Prism and Web Component DevTools Chrome extensions for property inspection

state-persistence.comment: No built-in persistence; implement via localStorage or third-party

time-travel-debugging.comment: Not built-in; available via Redux integration

official-cli.comment: No official CLI; recommends Vite templates or open-wc generator

hot-module-replacement.comment: Via Vite or other bundler integrations

browser-devtools.comment: Lit Prism and Web Component DevTools for Chrome

vs-code-extension.comment: lit-plugin extension provides syntax highlighting, type checking, and code completion

error-messages.comment: Clear errors with component context; template type checking via lit-plugin

documentation-quality.comment: Good documentation with guides, API reference, and examples; interactive playground

bundle-size-core.comment: ~5 kB minified + gzipped for core lit package

bundle-size-full.comment: ~16 kB minified for full lit with router and common utilities

virtual-dom.comment: No virtual DOM; uses efficient template part updates directly on DOM

compile-time-optimization.comment: Runtime-based; optional Lit Compiler for decorator optimization

code-splitting.comment: Via dynamic imports and bundler configuration

lazy-components.comment: Components can be dynamically imported and registered

streaming-ssr.comment: RenderResult supports streaming to HTTP responses

partial-hydration.comment: deferHydration option allows selective hydration of components

js-framework-benchmark.comment: Lit performs well in benchmarks; 8-10% faster than React VDOM in some tests

npm-weekly-downloads.comment: ~3.6 million weekly downloads as of December 2025

component-libraries.comment: Material Web, Shoelace/Web Awesome, Carbon Web Components, Spectrum Web Components, Vaadin, Lion, Patternfly, Calcite

meta-framework.comment: No official meta-framework; integrates with Astro, 11ty, and other SSG tools

major-companies-using.value: Adobe, Google, Reddit, Microsoft, Mozilla, Home Assistant, Internet Archive
major-companies-using.comment: Adobe Photoshop/Illustrator for Web, YouTube, Chrome DevTools, Firefox UI, Microsoft Store

official-sponsor-backer.comment: Originally Google project; joined OpenJS Foundation October 2025

discord-community-size.comment: Official Discord available but size not publicly disclosed

stack-overflow-questions.comment: Combined across lit, lit-element, and lit-html tags

job-market-demand.comment: Growing niche; web components expertise increasingly valued for design systems

testing-library-name.comment: open-wc testing helpers recommended; @lit-labs/testing for SSR

component-testing.comment: Via open-wc testing helpers and Web Test Runner

snapshot-testing.comment: Via Web Test Runner snapshot plugin

mobile-framework.comment: No official mobile framework; web components work in WebViews

pwa-support.comment: PWA Starter Kit template available; pwa-helpers library

desktop-framework.comment: No official desktop framework; works with Electron/Tauri via WebViews

learning-curve.comment: Relatively gentle; leverages web standards, familiar class-based components

concepts-to-learn.comment: Web Components, Shadow DOM, Reactive properties, Templates, Decorators, Lifecycle

migration-path.comment: Migration guides for Lit 2.x to 3.x and from Polymer

incremental-adoption.comment: Web components work anywhere HTML works; framework-agnostic
